I used to carry a lot of pens, and not worry too much about what pen I was using, but ever since I started just using one pen, I've realized that there's some cool things about using one pen. For example, if my pen is somewhere else, then I start to have more of an awareness of where that pen was, and if I have it on me, I feel a little bit more powerful than if I don't have it on me. And also, because I'm forced to just use this one pen all the time, I can come up with different things to do with the pen, you know, like a whistle graph. Would you like to come down the stairs, I don't want to, because I'm too scared. And I would almost say that the reason why I invented whistle graphs is because I chose to only stick to one pen. And then now that I'm sticking to one pen, I'm like, well, what is it that I can actually do with this thing? And so I think that just choosing to have one thing gives you both an awareness of that thing and makes you more creative.

clips.text: How do you quickly make a character look psychotic? It's easy. Just use the Kubrick stare. All it requires is two steps. First, tilt the head slightly downward. Second, stare forward through the eyebrows. It's a technique named after director Stanley Kubrick, who would often explore the concept of dehumanization in his films, stripping away the very essence of what made the character human. songs.title: ? here are my thoughts on written

clips.text: Here are my thoughts on written language. Even though the letters you write are two-dimensional, the lines you read are one-dimensional. This one-dimensionality is easy to demonstrate in this poem by Gertrude Stein. A rose is a rose is a rose is a rose is a rose. It doesn't matter what direction you enter from, you get stuck in the same linear loop and there's nothing magic about it. Contrast this with pictures which you can enter from any direction and have a nonlinear understanding of. Written language is a technology that wins at sequencing linear thought. But spatial thought is best explored in pictures. In conclusion, writing takes the guesswork or the spatial magic away from pictures. And magic is the key word here. Magic being the root word of image or imagination, which is a spatial thing that we have in our brains.

songs.title: ? how can you make art about

clips.text: How can you make art about feelings that you haven't sorted out yet? Well, one interesting thing about art is, you know, all art is basically about feeling and generally the best artwork is about feelings that are not so easily understood, you know. And when you're a little kid, you have a lot of energy and you can make a lot of really energetic artwork, but usually the feelings in that artwork are pretty simple and pretty reproducible. And that tends to be what we call the concept of cliché, which basically means a stamp, something that you can repeat like a meme. But when you get older, you're capable of having more complicated feelings and the best thing to do when you're having complicated feelings that you can't really put your finger on is to actually mediate them and make art about them and communicate them to others and that's basically how you make great work.

But if you really want to be a great director or a great auteur, then I would suggest that one of the first things you do is you actually sit down with an empty page and you start to draw. songs.title: ? generative art is kind of an

clips.text: Generative art is kind of an interesting topic because a lot of people I know would think of generative art as requiring a computer of some kind to draw little dots or to procedurally generate different kinds of things. However, generative art doesn't necessarily have to be using a computer. And actually you can do a lot of things with generative art that is very very difficult to do using a computer or by making software. For example, if you were to draw this square shape here, and then you wanted to transform the shape, you can do that very simply. You can just squash the shape. But what if you wanted to stretch the shape, right? You can stretch the shape like this. And these are basically the the ideas behind generative art. You don't really need to use a computer to draw cool lines and have them dancing around. You can actually just use the computer in your brain, so to speak, in order to develop new interesting forms in computations.

clips.text: If you're trying to figure out, like, what you should study in college or whatever, then maybe you should study art, because art's about total freedom. It's the ultimate safe space. It actually doesn't matter if you voted for Trump or if you hate sandwiches or love sandwiches or whatever. Basically, you can do whatever you want. And unlike science and technology, which kind of moves in one direction, going up in a curve, art and culture can kind of bounce around in all different places. You know, at some point, there could be a fascist who closes things down and bans homosexuality. At another point, you know, everybody could be having sex on the street and that would be okay. But art's basically about freedom and about moving culture where you want it to go. So if you have ideas about how you think the culture should be, then being an artist is kind of one of the best ways of, well, making those ideas actually reality.

songs.title: ? the most interesting drawing that you

clips.text: The most interesting drawing that you can make right now is a simple drawing of a line. Because once you draw a line, you might wonder why there's two sides to the line. And you might start to think about what that line might be like. Like is it thick or thin? Is the space around the line really big? Or is it really small? And once you can kind of understand what that line sort of means for yourself, you're also closer to understanding, well, yourself at that point in time. And the kinds of things that are going on in your mind, and the kinds of lines that you're drawing, or not drawing, inside.
